title: Easy Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix: Unnecessary Backtrack

statement.markdown: Given a Sudoku grid, fill in the missing numbers so that each row, column, and box (3x3) contains the numbers 1-9 without repeating any number.
statement.inputFormat: A 9x9 Sudoku grid represented as a 2D array of integers, where 0 indicates an empty cell.
statement.outputFormat: The modified Sudoku grid with all empty cells filled in.
statement.notes: The grid is guaranteed to be solvable.

constraints.description: Sudoku grid, where 0 indicates an empty cell

examples.explanation: The modified Sudoku grid with all empty cells filled in.

hints.text: The bug in the code is caused by an unnecessary backtrack call when a cell is already filled in.

editorial.title: Easy Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix
editorial.intuition: The goal of this problem is to fix a bug in the backtracking algorithm that is used to solve the Sudoku puzzle.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves trying all possible numbers in each cell until the puzzle is solved.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a backtracking algorithm that only explores valid possibilities.

functionSignature.cpp: bool isValid(int num, int i, int j, vector<vector<int>>& board);
functionSignature.java: boolean isValid(int num, int i, int j, int[][] board);
functionSignature.python: def isValid(self, num: int, i: int, j: int, board: List[List[int]]) -> bool;

solutions.python:
// Python code to solve the problem and print the result
s = Solution()
input_grid = [[5, 3, 0, 0, 7, 0, 0, 0, 0], [6, 0, 0, 1, 9, 5, 0, 0, 0], [0, 9, 8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 6, 0]]
result_grid = s.solve(input_grid)
print(result_grid)
